---
title: "Part 2: How to prepare a reproducible research package?"
authors:
  - name: Shunkei Kakimoto
    affiliations: "Department of Applied Economics, University of Minnesota"
format: 
  revealjs:
    html-math-method: mathjax
    embed-resources: true
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1600
    height: 1000
    theme: [simple, ../z_slide_style.scss]
    css: ../z_extra.css
    fontsize: 2em
    bibliography: ../../assets/z_reproducibility.bib
    callout-icon: false
    scrollable: true
    multiplex: false
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#447099"
---

## Materials for the workshop {.center}

All the slides and materials for this workshop are available from this GitHub repository (click the following link to access the repository): [**{{< fa brands github >}} Access the materials**](https://github.com/Shunkei3/Reproducibility-Workshop){target="_blank"}

<br>

There, you can find the slides for both Part 1 and Part 2, as well as the example reproducible projects with R and Stata that we will be using for the hands-on exercises.

## {{< fa list >}} Outline {.center}

+ [Reproducibility and Replicability](#review-reproducibility-and-replicability)
+ [Exercise: Examining an example of a reproducible Stata project](#example-of-a-reproducible-project-stata-version)
+ [Exercise: Evaluating other reproducible research projects in economics {.center}](#evaluating-other-reproducible-research-projects-in-economics)
  + [Example replication package 1: @Palma2022](#example1)
  + [Example replication package 2: @Pabilonia.VictoriaVernon2022](#example2)

<br>

::: {.callout-important}
## [{{< fa bullseye >}} Goal of today's session]{style="color:var(--red);"}
+ Through these hands-on exercises, you will gain a better understanding of how to implement reproducible practices in your own research projects.
:::

[Feel free to stop me at any time if you have questions or would like to share your thoughts.]{style="color:var(--light-maroon);"}

## Review: Reproducibility and Replicability

Reproducibility and replicability are often used interchangeably but have distinct meanings [@Cacioppo.etal2015].

<br>

::: {.callout-note}
### Definition: Reproducibility
"Reproducibility refers to the ability of a researcher to duplicate the results of a prior study using the same materials and procedures as were used by the original investigator."
:::
&rarr; Can we get the same results if we use the same data and code? 

<br>

:::{.fragment}
::: {.callout-note}
### Definition: Replicability
"Replicability refers to the ability of a researcher to duplicate the results of a prior study if the same procedures are followed but new data are collected.""
:::
&rarr; Can we get the same results if we collect new data and follow the same procedures?

<br>

[**Both reproducibility and replicability are essential for the robustness of scientific research.**]{style="color:var(--light-maroon);"}
:::


## Take a look at an example of a reproducible project (Stata version) {.center}

**Download the project material by cloning the following github repository**: 

Click [**{{< fa file-arrow-down >}} Download files**](https://github.com/Shunkei3/Sample-Reproducible-Project-stata){target="_blank"}

<br>

Or download the zip file from the repository and extract it to your local directory.

&rarr; Go to the repository > Click the green "Code" button > Click "Download ZIP" 

## Overview of the structure of the example project {.center}

This project provides code files to generate the analytical results and a manuscript file to compile the paper.

<br>

1. `code/main.do` is the master do-file that runs all the code files in the correct order.
2. `writing/manuscript_to_pdf.qmd` s the Quarto file that generates the manuscript PDF using the results produced by the code files.

<br>

::: {.callout-caution}
### Note
You do not need to use Quarto to write the manuscript. You can use any writing tool you prefer (e.g., R Markdown, LaTeX with Overleaf, or Word).
:::



## Example of a reproducible project (Stata version) {.center}

Pretend that the project you just downloaded is someone else’s project that you want to reproduce.

<br>

Let’s critically evaluate the reproducibility of the project by asking the following questions:

::: {.callout-note}
## Check:
1. Look at README.md. Does it clearly explain how to reproduce the results?
2. Look at the project structure. Is it organized in a way that facilitates reproducibility? Can you easily tell which code file corresponds to which part of the analysis?
3. Look at the code files. Do they follow the principle of an automated workflow?
4. What other features of this project do you think are helpful for reproducibility?
:::

<br>

::: {.callout-tip}
## Discussion
I am interested in hearing your thoughts on how to improve the workflow with Stata (e.g., How to manage the path to the working directory).
:::

## Evaluating other reproducible research projects in economics {.center}

There are several platforms where researchers share public data and code. **You can use these repositories as templates when preparing your own reproducible research packages (link attached)**.

1. [{{< fa link >}} AEA Data and Code Repository (OpenICPSR)](https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/sites/aea/home) — replication packages for economics journals

2. [{{< fa link >}} Harvard Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/) — widely used repository for data and code in economics

3. [{{< fa link >}} Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) — open repository with many downloadable replication packages

4. [{{< fa link >}} OSF](https://osf.io/search) — open platform for sharing research materials, data, and code

<br>

Let's examine a few real examples and discuss what makes them more or less reproducible.

## Example 1: @Palma2022 - "The Real Effects of Monetary Expansions: Evidence from a Large-scale Historical Experiment." {example1 .center}

+ Take a look at the replication package for the study by @Palma2022.
  + Here is the link to the replication package: [{{< fa link >}} Access the replication package](https://zenodo.org/records/5338362){target="_blank"}.
  + Here is the link to the original paper: [{{< fa link >}} Access the original paper](https://academic.oup.com/restud/article/89/3/1593/6375454?guestAccessKey=){target="_blank"}.

<br>

::: {.callout-note}
## Check:
+ Does the README file provide clear instructions on how to reproduce the results?
+ Can you easily tell which code files produce which tables, figures, or results?
+ Overall, do you think another researcher could reproduce the analysis without any difficulties? How would you improve the reproducibility of this project?
:::


## Example 2: @Pabilonia.VictoriaVernon2022 - "Telework, Wages, and Time Use in the United States" {#example2 .center}

+ Take a look at the replication package for the study by @Pabilonia.VictoriaVernon2022.
  + Here is the link to the replication package: [{{< fa link >}} Access the replication package](https://zenodo.org/records/7272406){target="_blank"}.
  + Here is the link to the original paper: [{{< fa link >}} Access the original paper](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11150-022-09601-1){target="_blank"}.

<br>

::: {.callout-note}
## Check:
+ Does the README file provide clear instructions on how to reproduce the results?
+ Can you easily tell which code files produce which tables, figures, or results?
+ Overall, do you think another researcher could reproduce the analysis without any difficulties? How would you improve the reproducibility of this project?
:::


## Key takeaways from this workshop {.center}

+ A small amount of extra effort can greatly improve the reproducibility of your research project.
+ Reproducible practices make your work easier to understand, check, and build on.
+ These practices benefit not only the scientific community, but also you and your collaborators.


 

## References {.center}
